generated_note: MACHINE PRE-VERIFICATION — NOT human verification. For each parameter, the primary citation's source text (open-access full text where available, otherwise the abstract) was fetched via Europe PMC and compared to the stored value. Every match/close/mismatch verdict carries a verbatim quote from the source so a human can confirm in seconds. This layer never sets extraction.review_status; promoting a parameter to 'verified' remains a human action after reading the source.

checks.source_reports: Bernstein 2001 reports plasma volume of 2320 +/- 280 mL (= 2.32 L) at 12 menstrual weeks, well below the stored central 3.05 L and below the stored low bound of 2.75 L.
checks.supporting_quote: Plasma volume at LH surge + 70 days (12 menstrual weeks, 2320 +/- 280 mL) was greater than either menstrual cycle estimates or early pregnancy estimates of plasma volume.
checks.note: Source 12-week plasma volume is 2.32 L; stored 3.05 L (range 2.75-3.35) is ~0.7 L higher and the source value falls below the stored range. Flag for human review.

checks.source_reports: Faught 1995 reports free protein S fell to a mean of 0.26 U/mL by the second trimester with no further fall in the third (so term ~0.26 U/mL ~= 26%). The stored 45% matches the FIRST-trimester mean (0.45 U/mL), not term; the source term value (~26%) falls below the stored range (30-60%).
checks.supporting_quote: Free protein S levels fell significantly from first to second trimesters (0.45 U/ml mean to 0.26 U/ml mean, p < 0.001), but no further fall occurred during the third trimester.
checks.note: Stored term value 45% equals the source's first-trimester mean (0.45 U/mL); the source's actual term (3rd-trimester) free protein S is ~0.26 U/mL (~26%), below the stored low bound of 30%. Flag for human review.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports 94.5 ml/min in the left ascending uterine artery before pregnancy (i.e. ~189 ml/min bilateral), far above the stored 50 ml/min.
checks.supporting_quote: a steady increase in volume flow rate in the left ascending uterine artery from a mean of 94.5 ml/min before pregnancy
checks.note: Stored 50 ml/min combined vs Thaler's 94.5 ml/min single-artery pre-pregnancy (~189 bilateral). Already flagged in dataset notes; ~3.8x discrepancy.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports the greatest CO increase as Δ0.6±1 l/min (by T2), markedly below the stored peak excess of 2.7 L/min.
checks.supporting_quote: The greatest increase in cardiac output occurred by the second trimester (Δ: 0.6 ± 1 l/min, P < 0.001)
checks.note: Stored 2.7 L/min peak excess vs abstract's Δ0.6±1 l/min for the same CO-rise quantity. Flagged in dataset notes for re-investigation against full results.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports mean amniotic fluid volume averaged 777 ml between 22 and 39 weeks (95% CI 302-1997 ml). It frames this as a flat average, not a peak, stating volume did not change significantly over that interval. The stored peak central 800 is not stated, but 777 lies within the stored range 600-1000.
checks.supporting_quote: mean amniotic fluid volume did not change significantly between 22 and 39 weeks and averaged 777 ml, with the 95% confidence interval ranging from 302 to 1997 ml.
checks.note: Abstract mean 777 ml falls within stored range 600-1000; but abstract describes a flat plateau, not a peak of 800. Per-week nomogram peak not in abstract.

checks.source_reports: Table 4: mean FHR baseline at >=37 weeks is 136.0-136.4 bpm (95% CI); weeks 37-42 range ~133-138 bpm. Normal range stated as 120-160 bpm.
checks.supporting_quote: Gestational age n 95% confidence interval A <28 1230 140.7538 – 141.9422 … >=37 8478 136.0104 – 136.4295
checks.note: Source term mean is ~136 bpm, just below stored 140 central but inside stored 130-150 range and the 120-160 normal range; magnitude/direction agree.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports HR increased maximally by the third trimester by Δ13±11 bpm; 13 falls within the stored 10–20 bpm range.
checks.supporting_quote: the heart rate increased maximally by the third trimester (Δ: 13 ± 11  bpm; P = 0.001)
checks.note: Reported Δ13±11 bpm vs stored 15 (range 10–20); same quantity and magnitude, central figure differs slightly.

checks.source_reports: Abstract states changes in plasma sodium accounted for the majority of the ~10 mOsm/kg osmolality decrement but gives no numeric sodium drop in mEq/L.
checks.supporting_quote: Changes in plasma sodium (and its attendant anion) accounted for the majority of the decrement.
checks.note: Abstract attributes most of the 10 mOsm/kg fall to sodium; a ~5 mEq/L drop is mechanistically consistent but no explicit sodium figure is stated.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports mean arterial PO2 consistently greater than 100 mmHg throughout pregnancy (106.4 at 12 wk falling to 101.8 at 38 wk); a non-pregnant baseline of ~100 mmHg is consistent with these values though not stated as a separate non-pregnant figure.
checks.supporting_quote: Mean arterial PO2 was consistently greater than 100 mm Hg throughout pregnancy, although the value decreased from 106.4 mm Hg at 12 weeks of gestation to 101.8 mm Hg at the 38th week.
checks.note: Abstract gives pregnant PaO2 values >100 mmHg; supports ~100 mmHg baseline magnitude but no explicit non-pregnant figure.

checks.source_reports: Abstract states MCA-PI was higher at 25-30 weeks (parabolic pattern peaking mid-gestation) but gives no numeric peak PI value.
checks.supporting_quote: The pulsatility index values of the middle cerebral artery were higher at 25 to 30 weeks' gestation
checks.note: Abstract confirms a mid-gestation peak at 25-30 wk consistent with stored ~28 wk peak, but the numeric value 2.0 is not stated.

checks.source_reports: Stirling 1984 names factor X among those that 'rose markedly throughout pregnancy', supporting the direction and magnitude class, but gives no percentage figure for the term value.
checks.supporting_quote: Factors VII, VIII:C, VIIIR:Ag, X, fibrinogen and alpha 1-antitrypsin, rose markedly throughout pregnancy.
checks.note: Factor X confirmed to rise markedly; stored 145% is plausible but the exact figure is not stated in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: de Haas 2017 meta-analysis reports a pooled maximum third-trimester increase of 1.13 L (45.6%) over the non-pregnant reference, not an absolute term plasma volume. 1.13 L on a ~2.7 L baseline is consistent with ~3.85 L absolute, but the absolute figure is not stated.
checks.supporting_quote: Plasma volume continued to increase in the third trimester with a pooled maximum increase of 1.13 L (95% CI, 1.07-1.19 L), an increase of 45.6% (95% CI, 43.0-48.1%) in physiological pregnancies compared with the reference value.
checks.note: Source gives the term INCREASE (1.13 L), consistent in magnitude with stored 3.85 L absolute, but does not state the absolute term volume.

checks.source_reports: Abstract states heart rate increased maximally by the third trimester; week 32 lies in T3, but no numeric peak week is given.
checks.supporting_quote: the heart rate increased maximally by the third trimester (Δ: 13 ± 11  bpm; P = 0.001)
checks.note: Abstract supports T3 timing of HR peak (week 32 is in T3); exact week not stated.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports 342 ml/min in the left ascending uterine artery in late gestation (~684 ml/min bilateral), near the stored combined 750 ml/min.
checks.supporting_quote: from a mean of 94.5 ml/min before pregnancy to a mean of 342 ml/min in late gestation (reflecting a 3.5-fold increase)
checks.note: Stored 750 ml/min combined vs ~684 ml/min from doubling Thaler's 342 ml/min single-artery term value; same magnitude.

checks.source_reports: Cortisol reached 352 +/- 90 ng/mL (= 35.2 ug/dL) at 26 weeks and 'changed minimally thereafter until labor.' During labor it reached 706 ng/mL (= 70.6 ug/dL). The plateau value 35.2 ug/dL falls within the stored range 25-40.
checks.supporting_quote: As pregnancy advanced, the concentration of cortisol in plasma increased progressively from 149 +/- 34 ng/ml (mean and SEM) at 12 weeks to 352 +/- 90 ng/ml at 26 weeks' gestation but changed minimally thereafter until labor commenced, during which values of 706 +/- 148 ng/ml were achieved.
checks.note: Pre-labor plateau ~352 ng/mL = 35.2 ug/dL falls in stored range 25-40; stored central 30 slightly below the abstract figure (unit conversion 10 ng/mL = 1 ug/dL).

checks.source_reports: Catalano 1991 reports a 56% decrease in insulin sensitivity by 36 weeks (i.e. insulin resistance roughly doubles), consistent in direction/magnitude with HOMA-IR rising from ~2 to ~4, but reports no HOMA-IR figure (uses clamp glucose infusion rate).
checks.supporting_quote: There was a significant (p = 0.0003) 56% decrease in insulin sensitivity through 36 weeks' gestation.
checks.note: 56% fall in insulin sensitivity supports ~2x rise in resistance (HOMA-IR 2->4) in direction/magnitude, but no HOMA-IR value reported.

checks.source_reports: Abstract states GFR increases 50% in pregnancy but gives no absolute term value in mL/min. A 50% rise on a ~100 mL/min baseline implies ~150 mL/min, consistent with stored, but the abstract supplies only the percentage, not the absolute figure.
checks.supporting_quote: The glomerular filtration rate increases 50% with subsequent decrease in serum creatinine, urea, and uric acid values.
checks.note: Abstract gives +50% rise only; stored 150 mL/min is consistent with 50% over ~100 baseline but the absolute number is inferred, not stated.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports ERPF increased 80% in early pregnancy. Applied to the stored ~600 mL/min baseline, +80% gives ~1080 mL/min; the stored peak of 900 mL/min (+50%) is within the stored high bound (1050) but lower than the 80% rise the abstract emphasizes. No absolute peak value is stated.
checks.supporting_quote: Compared with non-pregnant values, ERPF increased by 80 percent during early pregnancy but fell significantly from this new level during the third trimester.
checks.note: Abstract supports a large early-pregnancy RPF rise (~80%); stored 900 mL/min is the same magnitude but no absolute peak number is given, and +80% would imply higher (~1080).

checks.source_reports: Abstract states ERPF rose in early pregnancy and fell in the third trimester, implying a mid-pregnancy peak, but gives no specific peak week.
checks.supporting_quote: ERPF increased by 80 percent during early pregnancy but fell significantly from this new level during the third trimester.
checks.note: Abstract implies a mid-pregnancy RPF peak (rise early, fall in T3) consistent with ~24 weeks, but no explicit peak week is reported.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext states FRC decreases by 9.5-25% at term but gives no absolute liter value, so the stored 2.0 L central cannot be confirmed numerically; only the directional/percent fall is supported.
checks.supporting_quote: FRC then decreases (by 9.5–25%) while inspiratory capacity increases at the same rate in order to maintain stable TLC
checks.note: Source supports an FRC fall (~9.5-25%) but no absolute 2.0 L value; percent-only claim.

checks.source_reports: Review reports minute ventilation increases significantly (by up to 48%) in the first trimester and is then maintained, driven by tidal-volume rise with unchanged respiratory rate. No absolute term value (e.g. ~10.5 L/min) is stated; only a percent change is given.
checks.supporting_quote: Minute ventilation ( V ′ E ) starts to increase significantly (by up to 48%) during the first trimester of gestation, due to higher tidal volume ( V T ) with unchanged respiratory rate. This ventilatory pattern is then maintained throughout the course of pregnancy
checks.note: Source gives a percent increase (up to 48%) not the absolute 10.5 L/min; direction/magnitude consistent. Confirm percent-only claim.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext states oxygen consumption increases by up to 21% in pregnancy but gives no absolute mL/min value, so the stored 300 mL/min term central cannot be confirmed numerically; only the percent rise is supported.
checks.supporting_quote: Oxygen consumption and basal metabolic rate increase (by up to 21% and 14%, respectively), but to a lesser extent than ventilatory augmentation
checks.note: Source gives ~21% O2-consumption rise, not absolute 300 mL/min; percent-only claim.

checks.source_reports: Hoddick abstract states placental thickness increases with menstrual age and the normal placenta never exceeded 4 cm; supports the upper bound but does not state a term central value of 2.5 cm.
checks.supporting_quote: At no stage of pregnancy was the normal placenta greater than 4 cm in thickness.
checks.note: Abstract supports the normal upper bound (<4 cm, stored high 3.5) and the increasing trend; the central 2.5 cm itself is not stated.

checks.source_reports: Kruithof 1987 reports PAI-1 antigen rose from 54 +/- 17 ng/mL (non-pregnant) to 144 +/- 25 ng/mL at term. The source term value (144) is higher than the stored central (80) but falls within the stored range (40-150).
checks.supporting_quote: its antigen level, measured by a radioimmunoassay, increased from 54 +/- 17 ng/mL to 144 +/- 25 ng/mL.
checks.note: Source term PAI-1 antigen ~144 ng/mL vs stored central 80; same magnitude and 144 lies within stored range 40-150, but stored central is notably lower.

checks.source_reports: Abstract states most maternally administered cortisol was metabolized to cortisone (qualitative), with no numeric inactivation fraction reported.
checks.supporting_quote: Most of the maternally administered cortisol was metabolized to cortisone
checks.note: 'Most' is qualitatively consistent with the stored 0.85 (0.7-0.95) but the abstract gives no percentage; verdict close, not match.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext gives fetal/maternal IgG as 5-10% at weeks 17-22 rising to 50% at weeks 28-32; the stored mid-pregnancy central 0.2 (range 0.1-0.4) falls within this rising trajectory but is not stated as a single figure.
checks.supporting_quote: Fetal IgG concentrations were only 5%–10% of the maternal levels at weeks 17–22 but reached 50% of the maternal concentrations at weeks 28–32.
checks.note: Source brackets mid-pregnancy ratio (0.05-0.10 at wk17-22; 0.50 at wk28-32); stored 0.2 (0.1-0.4) sits within the rising trajectory but is not a stated figure.

checks.source_reports: The standard P50 constant 26.6 appears verbatim in Severinghaus eq 4 (the Bohr-coefficient equation), where it is the reference Po2 at half-saturation.
checks.supporting_quote: delta In Po2/delta pH = (Po2/26.6)(0.184) - 2.2 (4)
checks.note: 26.6 appears verbatim as the standard P50 constant embedded in eq 4; consistent with stored 26.6 mmHg.

checks.source_reports: Abstract explicitly reports plasma osmolality fell to 10 mOsm/kg below preconception by the tenth week (and 9 mOsm/kg lower in the last trimester vs postpartum), matching the stored 10 mOsm/kg drop.
checks.supporting_quote: plasma osmolality (Posm) … was 10 mosmol/kg lower than preconception values by the tenth week, changing little thereafter.
checks.note: Direct verbatim match: 10 mOsm/kg drop stated explicitly in the Davison 1981 abstract.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext states arterial carbon dioxide tension plateaus around 32 mmHg (alveolar around 27 mmHg) at term, exactly matching the stored central of 32 mmHg (range 30-34).
checks.supporting_quote: alveolar and arterial carbon dioxide tension ( P CO 2 ) levels decrease to plateaux around 27 and 32 mmHg, respectively
checks.note: Arterial PaCO2 plateau 32 mmHg quoted verbatim, matches stored central 32. (Source also mentions ~30 mmHg in a clinical box.)

checks.source_reports: Fulltext states arterial oxygen tension reaches 101-104 mmHg in the third trimester; stored central 103 mmHg (range 101-105) falls inside this window.
checks.supporting_quote: arterial oxygen tension increases, reaching 106–108 mmHg and 101–104 mmHg in the first and third trimesters, respectively
checks.note: Third-trimester 101-104 mmHg quoted verbatim; stored 103 sits within it.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext explicitly states hCG reaches a peak at 10 weeks of gestation, matching the stored central value of 10 weeks (range 8-12).
checks.supporting_quote: As shown in Tables 2 and 3 hCG reaches a peak at 10 weeks of gestation, or almost one month after progesterone promotion is complete, then continues to be produced through the length of pregnancy.
checks.note: Confirm the verbatim quote 'hCG reaches a peak at 10 weeks of gestation' supports the stored central value of 10 weeks.

checks.source_reports: Shunt fraction reduced from 30% to 20% across the second half of pregnancy; 20% is the late-pregnancy endpoint.
checks.supporting_quote: The mean fraction of umbilical blood shunted through the ductus is reduced from 30% to 20% during the second half of the human pregnancy
checks.note: Stored 0.20 matches the 20% late-pregnancy endpoint stated in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: Mean fraction of umbilical blood shunted through the ductus reduced from 30% to 20% during the second half of pregnancy; the 30% is the earlier (mid-pregnancy) value.
checks.supporting_quote: The mean fraction of umbilical blood shunted through the ductus is reduced from 30% to 20% during the second half of the human pregnancy
checks.note: Stored 0.30 matches the 30% mid-pregnancy starting fraction stated in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: Faught 1995 found no statistically significant change in protein C during normal pregnancy, consistent with the stored ~100% (unchanged) term value.
checks.supporting_quote: There was no statistically significant change in antigenic or functional protein C levels during normal pregnancy.
checks.note: Source explicitly reports protein C unchanged in pregnancy, supporting stored 100%.

checks.source_reports: Abstract states a rise in aldosterone produces a net gain of approximately 1000 mg of sodium, equal to the stored 1.0 g cumulative sodium retention.
checks.supporting_quote: A rise in serum aldosterone results in a net gain of approximately 1000 mg of sodium.
checks.note: Direct match: 1000 mg = 1.0 g stated explicitly in the Cheung 2013 abstract.

checks.source_reports: Kruithof 1987 reports PAI-2 rose from below the detection limit (~10 ng/mL) to 260 ng/mL at term, consistent with the stored central 250 ng/mL (within rounding and the stored range 150-400).
checks.supporting_quote: Its level, quantified with a radioimmunoassay, increased from below the detection limit (approximately 10 ng/mL) in normal plasma to 260 ng/mL at term.
checks.note: Source term PAI-2 of 260 ng/mL closely matches stored 250 ng/mL.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext states fetal IgG concentrations usually exceed maternal ones by 20-30% at full term, i.e. a fetal/maternal ratio of ~1.2-1.3, consistent with the stored central 1.2 (range 1.0-1.5).
checks.supporting_quote: fetal IgG concentrations usually exceed maternal ones by 20%–30% at full term
checks.note: 20-30% excess => ratio 1.2-1.3; stored central 1.2 matches the lower bound of the source statement.

checks.source_reports: Abstract does not report a 20-week AFV. Its only quantitative summary covers the 22-39 week window (mean 777 ml). The 20-week point would come from the nomogram, which is not in the abstract.
checks.note: No 20-week value in abstract (covers 22-39 wk only). Requires fulltext nomogram.

checks.source_reports: Abstract gives no gestational week of peak AFV. It states volume did not change significantly between 22 and 39 weeks, which does not support a distinct peak at week 33. The week-by-week nomogram is not in the abstract.
checks.note: No peak-week figure in abstract; abstract states no significant change 22-39 wk. Need fulltext/nomogram to verify week 33.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports no term-specific value; it averages 22-39 weeks as 777 ml with no significant change. A distinct decline to ~600 ml at term is not in the abstract (would require the nomogram).
checks.note: No term-specific AFV in abstract; abstract reports flat 777 ml mean 22-39 wk. Term value requires fulltext nomogram.

checks.source_reports: Abstract describes constructing reference ranges/percentiles for UA-PI showing continuous reduction through the second half of pregnancy, but gives no numeric PI value for 16-20 weeks.
checks.note: No numeric UA-PI figure (~1.5 at mid-T2) in the abstract; reference-range numbers are in tables/full text not available here.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports a continuous reduction in UA-PI through the second half of pregnancy without plateau near term, but no numeric term PI value.
checks.note: No numeric term UA-PI (~0.85) in the abstract; only qualitative description of the declining trend.

checks.source_reports: Abstract describes a regression-based in utero weight model but does not print the equation or its intercept; it states the resulting weight range (35 g at 10w to 3,619 g at 40w, SD +/-12.7%).
checks.supporting_quote: Regression analysis was used to develop an in utero fetal weight model from a population of 392 predominantly middle-class white patients … There was a gradual increase in fetal weight from 35 g at 10 weeks to 3,619 g at 40 weeks, with uniform variance of +/- 12.7% (1 standard deviation) throughout gestation.
checks.note: The intercept 1.3596 is the published Hadlock-III formula constant but does not appear in the abstract; abstract confirms the model exists and its weight range but not the coefficient itself.

checks.source_reports: Abstract gives the dissociation equation S = (((Po2^3 + 150 Po2)^-1 x 23,400) + 1)^-1 but never prints a Hill coefficient of 2.7; the ~2.7 exponent is inferred from the cubic numerator, not stated.
checks.note: Hill coefficient 2.7 is derivable from the cubic form but no verbatim '2.7' appears in the available abstract.

checks.source_reports: Dash & Bassingthwaighte 2010 abstract describes invertible Hill-type equations for HbO2/HbCO2 but does not print a Bohr coefficient value of -0.48.
checks.note: No numeric Bohr coefficient (-0.48) appears in the available abstract; the rationale itself concedes Dash 2010 does not print the coefficient.

checks.source_reports: The abstract reports cortisol levels during pregnancy only (149 ng/mL = 14.9 ug/dL at 12 weeks, rising to 352 ng/mL at 26 weeks). No non-pregnant (baseline) cortisol value is given.
checks.note: Carr 1981 abstract gives no non-pregnant baseline cortisol; earliest figure is 12-week pregnant (149 ng/mL = 14.9 ug/dL).

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports the percentage rise of GFR and RPF in pregnancy but gives no absolute non-pregnant GFR baseline value.
checks.supporting_quote: Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) and renal plasma flow (RPF) increase by 40-65% and 50-85%, respectively, during normal pregnancy in women.
checks.note: Conrad 2001 abstract gives only percentage increases; the absolute non-pregnant baseline of 100 mL/min is not stated in the cached source.

checks.source_reports: Abstract notes a decrease in serum urea with the GFR rise but reports no numeric BUN value.
checks.supporting_quote: The glomerular filtration rate increases 50% with subsequent decrease in serum creatinine, urea, and uric acid values.
checks.note: Abstract confirms urea falls in pregnancy but gives no numeric term BUN; stored 8 mg/dL not verifiable from abstract.

checks.source_reports: Abstract notes a decrease in serum creatinine with the GFR rise but reports no numeric creatinine value. The 0.5 mg/dL Table 1 figure cited in the rationale is from the fulltext, which is not cached.
checks.supporting_quote: The glomerular filtration rate increases 50% with subsequent decrease in serum creatinine, urea, and uric acid values.
checks.note: Abstract confirms direction (creatinine falls) but no numeric term creatinine; specific 0.6 mg/dL not verifiable from abstract.

checks.source_reports: Abstract covers blood-gases, A-a gradient, shunt and VD/VT but does not report an arterial pH value; the non-pregnant pH 7.40 baseline is not in the available abstract text.
checks.note: Templeton 1976 abstract reports no pH value; baseline pH 7.40 not verifiable from available text.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext states respiratory rate is unchanged through pregnancy (the ventilation rise is tidal-volume driven) but gives no absolute breaths/min figure, so the stored 16 breaths/min central is not numerically confirmed.
checks.supporting_quote: higher tidal volume ( V T ) with unchanged respiratory rate
checks.note: Source confirms RR unchanged but states no absolute ~16 breaths/min baseline value.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext discusses tidal-volume rise as the driver of increased minute ventilation but gives no absolute non-pregnant tidal-volume value (~450 mL). The specific baseline figure is not present.
checks.note: No absolute non-pregnant tidal volume (~450 mL) stated in the available fulltext.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext states respiratory rate is unchanged through pregnancy (ventilatory pattern maintained), supporting that term RR equals baseline, but gives no absolute breaths/min figure for the stored 16.
checks.supporting_quote: higher tidal volume ( V T ) with unchanged respiratory rate. This ventilatory pattern is then maintained throughout the course of pregnancy
checks.note: Source confirms RR unchanged at term but states no absolute ~16 breaths/min value.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext confirms hCG peaks at 10 weeks of gestation, but does not state the peak maternal serum concentration in mIU/mL. The only mIU/mL figures in the text concern pituitary hCG during the menstrual cycle (mean 1.54 mIU/mL); Tables 2/3 give term-pregnancy total-hCG ranges in ng/ml (0.21-173 ng/ml; 1.86-1308 ng/ml), not the ~50,000-200,000 mIU/mL peak.
checks.note: Confirm the peak hCG concentration (50,000-200,000 mIU/mL) is not in the captured fulltext (lives in figures/tables not extracted as text); peak value appears sourced from general reproductive endocrinology, not an explicit number in this paper.

checks.source_reports: Naeye abstract reports analysis of cord length in 35,779 neonates and that the normal range is large, but states no mean (~55 cm) or short/long thresholds (35/80 cm).
checks.note: Abstract confirms the cohort but gives no numeric mean or range; central 55 cm and 35-80 cm bounds not in available text.

checks.source_reports: Abstract is a generic review summary with no numeric values; it does not report an AF creatinine concentration at term.
checks.note: Underwood 2005 abstract gives no numbers. Term AF creatinine 2 mg/dL would require the review fulltext.

checks.source_reports: Abstract is a generic review summary with no numeric values; it does not report an AF glucose concentration at term.
checks.note: Underwood 2005 abstract gives no numbers. Term AF glucose 0.5 mmol/L would require the review fulltext.

checks.source_reports: Abstract is a generic review summary with no numeric values; it does not report an AF lactate concentration at term.
checks.note: Underwood 2005 abstract gives no numbers. Term AF lactate 4 mmol/L would require the review fulltext.

checks.source_reports: Abstract is a generic review summary with no numeric values; it does not report an AF osmolality at term.
checks.note: Underwood 2005 abstract gives no numbers. Term AF osmolality 260 mOsm/kg would require the review fulltext.

checks.source_reports: Abstract states the data span 8 to 43 weeks but provides no early-pregnancy (~12-14 wk) volume figure. The 50-200 ml early baseline is not quantified in the abstract.
checks.note: No early-pregnancy AFV figure in abstract. Requires fulltext nomogram to verify ~100 ml baseline.

checks.source_reports: Sutton 1991 abstract computes CVO as the sum of aortic and pulmonary artery flows and expresses lung/foramen flows as fractions of CVO, but reports no absolute per-kg CVO value (mL/min/kg).
checks.note: Abstract gives CVO only as a denominator for fraction calculations; no ~450 mL/min/kg numeric value present.

checks.source_reports: This study analyzed CTG tracings collected between 20 and 42 weeks; it reports the highest mean FHR baseline at <28 weeks (140.8-141.9 bpm) and a normal range of 120-160 bpm. It does not cover the first-trimester (~weeks 9-10) peak that the stored 170 bpm represents.
checks.note: Source data start at 20 weeks; the first-trimester ~170 bpm peak is outside this study and not present in the text.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports a parabolic (bell-shaped) MCA-PI pattern with higher values at 25-30 weeks, but provides no numeric PI value for ~20 weeks.
checks.note: No numeric early-T2 MCA-PI (~1.5) in the abstract; only the parabolic-pattern description.

checks.source_reports: Sutton 1991 abstract reports lung flow (22% of CVO) and foramen ovale flow (17-31% of CVO) but does not report the RV share of CVO; no ~60% RV-output fraction is stated.
checks.note: Abstract does not state RV vs LV output split; stored 0.60 RV fraction not verifiable from available text.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports shunt fractions and growth associations only; no weight-normalised UV flow (mL/min/kg) value is given.
checks.note: No mL/min/kg figure in the abstract; the ~80 mL/min/kg term value is not in available text.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports DV shunt fractions (28-32% at 18-20 wk down to 18% at 31 wk) and growth associations, but gives no absolute umbilical-vein flow value in mL/min.
checks.note: Abstract focuses on shunt fraction; the absolute ~290 mL/min term UV flow is not stated in available text.

checks.source_reports: Faught 1995 measured only protein C and protein S; antithrombin is not mentioned in the abstract.
checks.note: Primary citation (Faught 1995) does not report antithrombin; the ~95% term value cannot be verified against this source.

checks.source_reports: Kline 2005 abstract reports preconception mean D-dimer 0.43 mg/L and that none of 23 third-trimester women had D-dimer <0.50 mg/L, but gives no third-trimester median value (the stored 1.5 ug/mL).
checks.note: Direction (monotonic rise, third-trimester all >0.50) is confirmed, but no quantitative term median ~1.5 ug/mL is stated in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: Stirling 1984 abstract lists factors VII, VIII, X and fibrinogen as rising markedly but does not mention factor IX at all.
checks.note: Factor IX is not named in the abstract; the ~130% term value cannot be verified.

checks.source_reports: Stirling 1984 abstract does not mention factor XII; only VII, VIII, X, fibrinogen, II, V and several inhibitors are named.
checks.note: Factor XII is not named in the abstract; the ~160% term value cannot be verified.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports only the change in cardiac output (Δ0.6 l/min by T2), not an absolute pre-conception baseline.
checks.note: Abstract gives deltas, not the absolute pre-conception CO; baseline 4.6 not stated in available text.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports HR change (Δ13±11 bpm) only; no absolute pre-conception baseline HR value.
checks.note: Only HR delta in abstract; absolute baseline 70 bpm not stated.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports systolic BP changes (deltas) only; no absolute pre-conception MAP value given.
checks.note: No absolute MAP in abstract; only delta brachial/central systolic. Baseline 85 unverifiable from text.

checks.source_reports: Abstract states the greatest CO increase occurred by the second trimester; no explicit peak week ~30 reported.
checks.note: Abstract locates greatest CO rise at T2, not a numeric peak week of 30; specific value not in text.

checks.source_reports: Abstract gives only the CO change (Δ0.6 l/min) and no absolute trimester-stratified CO means.
checks.note: Absolute T1 CO (5.5) not in abstract; only deltas reported.

checks.source_reports: Abstract gives only the CO change (Δ0.6 l/min greatest by T2); no absolute T2 CO mean.
checks.note: Absolute T2 CO (6.4) not in abstract; only the delta is reported.

checks.source_reports: Abstract gives only the CO change; no absolute T3 CO mean.
checks.note: Absolute T3 CO (6.5) not in abstract.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports systolic BP drops (brachial 4±7, central 7±7 mmHg) in early pregnancy, not a MAP nadir drop.
checks.note: Stored quantity is MAP drop (8 mmHg); abstract reports systolic-BP deltas, a different quantity. MAP drop not stated.

checks.source_reports: Abstract says greatest BP reduction occurred in early pregnancy and PVR nadir by T2; no explicit MAP-nadir week of 22.
checks.note: No explicit week-22 MAP nadir in abstract; timing described qualitatively only.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports HR increased maximally by T3 by Δ13±11 bpm; no absolute term HR (~85 bpm) is stated.
checks.note: Term 85 bpm is baseline+delta inference; abstract gives only the delta, not absolute term HR.

checks.source_reports: The Carr 1981 abstract discusses ACTH and total cortisol only; it never mentions cortisol-binding globulin (CBG) or transcortin and gives no CBG concentration.
checks.note: CBG not mentioned anywhere in the Carr 1981 abstract; no value to compare against stored 70 mg/L.

checks.source_reports: Catalano 1991 abstract measures insulin sensitivity via hyperinsulinemic-euglycemic clamp (glucose infusion rate) and insulin release via IVGTT. It reports no HOMA-IR value and no pre-pregnancy baseline HOMA-IR figure.
checks.note: Abstract reports clamp-based insulin sensitivity, not HOMA-IR; no baseline HOMA-IR value stated.

checks.source_reports: Cheung 2013 abstract does not mention filtration fraction or give any numeric FF value.
checks.note: No filtration-fraction data in the Cheung 2013 abstract; stored 0.18 not verifiable from cached source.

checks.source_reports: Abstract describes mechanisms of hyperfiltration but gives no gestational timing for the GFR peak.
checks.note: No week-of-peak GFR information in the Conrad 2001 abstract; Dunlop 1981 (also cited in rationale) is not the primary citation here.

checks.source_reports: Abstract notes uric acid decreases with the GFR rise but gives no numeric nadir value or timing.
checks.supporting_quote: The glomerular filtration rate increases 50% with subsequent decrease in serum creatinine, urea, and uric acid values.
checks.note: Abstract confirms uric acid falls but no numeric mid-pregnancy nadir; stored 3.0 mg/dL not verifiable from abstract.

checks.source_reports: Abstract notes a pregnancy decrease in uric acid but provides no numeric term value nor any third-trimester rebound figure.
checks.supporting_quote: The glomerular filtration rate increases 50% with subsequent decrease in serum creatinine, urea, and uric acid values.
checks.note: No numeric term uric acid in the Cheung 2013 abstract; stored 4.5 mg/dL not verifiable from cached source.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports ERPF increased 80% in early pregnancy but provides no absolute non-pregnant ERPF value in mL/min.
checks.supporting_quote: Compared with non-pregnant values, ERPF increased by 80 percent during early pregnancy but fell significantly from this new level during the third trimester.
checks.note: Abstract gives percentage change only; absolute baseline RPF of 600 mL/min not stated in cached source.

checks.source_reports: Cheung 2013 abstract does not mention urinary protein excretion or proteinuria thresholds.
checks.note: No urinary protein data in the Cheung 2013 abstract; stored 150 mg/24h not verifiable from cached source.

checks.source_reports: Templeton 1976 abstract reports no pH value. (The LoMauro review fulltext states blood pH stays at slightly alkalotic values 7.40-7.47, which would bracket 7.44, but that is not the primary citation for this parameter.)
checks.note: Primary citation Templeton 1976 abstract reports no pH; term pH 7.44 not verifiable from that source's available text.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext reports basal metabolic rate (and O2 consumption) rises by up to 14%/21% but gives no CO2-production (VCO2) value, absolute or percent, so the stored 250 mL/min term VCO2 is not present.
checks.note: No CO2-production (VCO2) figure in available fulltext; only O2 consumption/metabolic-rate percentages are given.

checks.source_reports: Fulltext does not state a term maternal serum hCG concentration of ~10,000 mIU/mL. Term-pregnancy hCG data in Tables 2/3 are described as ng/ml ranges (0.21-173 ng/ml across women) and the underlying per-week table values are not present in the captured text.
checks.note: Confirm the term hCG figure (~5,000-20,000 mIU/mL) is absent from the captured fulltext; it appears inferred from the general decline-from-peak narrative rather than an explicit number.

checks.source_reports: The abstract is qualitative and states no numeric non-pregnant baseline concentration. The operationally-zero baseline rests on hPL being exclusively placental, which the abstract supports descriptively but without a number or assay sensitivity figure.
checks.note: Confirm the abstract gives no numeric baseline; the 0 (below ~0.1 ug/mL assay sensitivity) value is an operational inference, not an explicit figure in this source.

checks.source_reports: The abstract is qualitative and describes hPL's physiologic roles (growth/metabolism in mother and fetus). It states no numeric term concentration in ug/mL.
checks.note: Confirm the abstract carries no numeric hPL concentration; the ~5-10 ug/mL term value would need the full review text or another source.

checks.source_reports: The abstract reports leptin was elevated throughout gestation (P<0.05), especially in the second trimester, and fell sharply post-partum, but it gives no numeric leptin concentration (ng/mL) at term.
checks.note: Confirm the abstract carries only directional/statistical findings and no numeric term leptin value; the ~30 ng/mL central value would require the paper's tables/figures.

checks.source_reports: The abstract (a renal-vasodilation/hyperfiltration review) identifies relaxin as the major pregnancy hormone driving renal changes and describes its downstream mediators (ET, NO), but reports no serum relaxin concentration. It gives GFR/RPF increases (40-65% and 50-85%), not a relaxin level.
checks.note: Confirm the abstract states no relaxin concentration; the ~1 ng/mL first-trimester value is not supported by this mechanistic-review abstract and may need a dedicated relaxin assay source.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reviews factors affecting placental oxygen transfer in general/evolutionary terms; it does not state a maternal intervillous-space PO2 value.
checks.note: No PO2 figure in abstract; ~50 mmHg claim not verifiable from available text. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reviews placental oxygen transfer; it does not report any umbilical-artery PCO2 value.
checks.note: No UA PCO2 figure in abstract. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reviews placental oxygen-transfer factors; it does not report an umbilical-artery PO2 value.
checks.note: No UA PO2 figure in abstract. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reviews placental oxygen transfer; it does not report any umbilical-vein PCO2 value.
checks.note: No UV PCO2 figure in abstract. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reviews placental oxygen-transfer factors and globin/oxygen-affinity evolution; it does not report an umbilical-vein PO2 value.
checks.note: No UV PO2 figure in abstract. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: The abstract is a qualitative review of placental GLUT isoform localization, regulation, and functional significance. It does not report any numeric Michaelis constant (Km) for GLUT1.
checks.note: Abstract-only; no numeric Km in available text. The '2-5 mmol/L' claim lives in the dataset rationale, not the cited abstract.

checks.source_reports: The Baumann 2002 abstract reviews placental glucose transfer; it states only GLUT1 protein has been identified in the syncytium and gives no numeric Km for GLUT3.
checks.supporting_quote: only GLUT1 protein has been identified in the syncytium, where its distribution is asymmetric.
checks.note: Abstract reports no GLUT3 Km figure; it actually emphasizes GLUT1. Stored '~1-2 mmol/L' Km is not present in the available text.

checks.source_reports: The Illsley 2000 abstract does not state a maternal-fetal glucose concentration gradient or a fetal/maternal glucose ratio; it discusses transporter biology qualitatively.
checks.note: Abstract-only; no numeric gradient (~1.0-1.5 mmol/L) or 70-75% ratio appears in the available text.

checks.source_reports: The abstract mentions 'maternal-fetal flux of glucose' only qualitatively; it reports no numeric net trans-placental glucose flux (e.g. 4-7 mg/kg/min).
checks.supporting_quote: in diabetic pregnancies increases in basal GLUT1 expression and activity have been observed, with significant consequences for the maternal-fetal flux of glucose.
checks.note: Abstract references maternal-fetal glucose flux qualitatively but gives no mg/kg/min value. Stored 4-7 mg/kg/min not in available text.

checks.source_reports: Morphomics review abstract describes methodology and 3D quantification but reports no early-gestation villous surface-area figure.
checks.note: Abstract is a methodological 'omics' review; no numeric villous surface area present.

checks.source_reports: Morphomics review abstract; reports no villous-membrane thickness value.
checks.note: No numeric membrane-thickness figure in the abstract; underlying value attributed to Mayhew 1986, not this cached source.

checks.source_reports: Burton 'What is the placenta?' abstract; qualitative organ description, reports no placental-to-fetal weight ratio.
checks.note: No fetoplacental ratio value in the abstract; stored value is a derived/computed quantity.

checks.source_reports: Morphomics review abstract; no term villous surface-area figure present.
checks.note: Abstract does not state the ~11-13 m^2 term surface area; value would require fulltext or the cited primary sources.

checks.source_reports: Morphomics review abstract; reports no placental mass value.
checks.note: Rationale itself calls ~470 g a 'canonical clinical-pathology figure'; not present in this abstract.

checks.source_reports: This is an empirical Gaussian-fit spread (sigma) parameter derived downstream, not a quantity Brace & Wolf report. The abstract contains no such curve-shape parameter.
checks.note: Derived Gaussian-fit parameter, not reported by the source; abstract has no curve sigma. Per rationale it is a single-fit modeling value.

checks.source_reports: Mari 1995 abstract reports MCA pulsatility-index waveforms only; it gives no absolute MCA flow value in mL/min.
checks.note: Abstract is about MCA-PI, not absolute flow; the ~30 mL/min term MCA flow is not in available text.

checks.source_reports: Kiserud DV review abstract discusses shunt fractions (30%->20%) and DV regulation but reports no PO2 figure for systemic venous return.
checks.note: Abstract contains no PO2/oxygen-tension value; the 15 mmHg systemic-venous-return PO2 is not in the available text.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports that in appropriate-for-gestational-age fetuses plasma insulin increased exponentially with gestation, but gives no numeric central plasma insulin value (no pmol/L figure) for term.
checks.note: Abstract available but contains no specific numeric insulin value to compare against stored 50 pmol/L; qualitative trends only.

checks.source_reports: Abstract does not report an absolute stroke-volume value; baseline SV is a CO/HR-derived estimate.
checks.note: No SV figure in abstract; stored 65 mL is a derivation per rationale.

checks.source_reports: Abstract describes the qualitative shape of cardiac adaptation; it reports no between-individual sigma figure.
checks.note: Qualitative review; 13% individual sigma is a model-fit abstraction, not in source.

checks.source_reports: Abstract describes timing qualitatively (HR rises 2–5 weeks, SV later) but reports no Gaussian spread parameter.
checks.note: Spread (weeks) is a curve-fit parameter; not reported in the qualitative abstract.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports flow means at sampled timepoints, not a logistic growth-rate parameter.
checks.note: 0.22/week is a fit parameter; growth-rate constant not reported in abstract.

checks.source_reports: Abstract reports population means; it does not report a fractional between-individual sigma in the available text.
checks.note: Fractional sigma (0.2) is model-fit; not stated in abstract.

checks.source_reports: Abstract contains no logistic-fit rate parameter; this is an empirical model-fit value, not reported by the source.
checks.note: Logistic rate is a derived curve-fit parameter; not present in the Conrad 2001 abstract.

checks.source_reports: Cheung 2013 abstract does not mention phosphate handling or any TmP/GFR value.
checks.note: No phosphate-threshold data in the Cheung 2013 abstract; stored 0.85 mmol/L not verifiable from cached source.

checks.source_reports: The abstract describes the qualitative shift from episodic pituitary GH to continuous non-pulsatile placental GH-variant secretion in late pregnancy (first seen at 17 weeks), but reports no numeric term placental-GH concentration in ng/mL.
checks.note: Confirm the abstract gives only the secretion-pattern finding and no numeric concentration; the ~10-20 ng/mL term value needs the full text or another source.

checks.source_reports: Abstract estimates the oxygen-diffusing capacity of the term placenta morphometrically but reports no numeric value and does not give a CO2 diffusing capacity. Rationale notes the CO2 value is back-calculated by analogy to O2.
checks.note: Abstract gives no numeric diffusing-capacity value and does not address CO2. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: Abstract describes a morphometric diffusing-capacity model and ranks the influence of villous-membrane thickness, villous and capillary surface areas, but gives no numeric half-saturation surface area. Rationale states the half-saturation area is an explicit model-fit abstraction, not directly reported.
checks.note: Phenomenological model-fit parameter; abstract has no numeric villous-area value. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: Abstract is an evolutionary review of invasive placentation in primates; it contains no fraction or equilibration figure. Rationale states the value is a model abstraction derived to reproduce the textbook UV PO2 window.
checks.note: Model abstraction; cited abstract is qualitative evolutionary review with no numeric value. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: Abstract is a morphometric oxygen-diffusion model; it does not discuss CO2 or report any maternal-fetal PCO2 gradient. Rationale itself attributes the value to 'standard fetal-physiology references.'
checks.note: Cited abstract is O2-only; no PCO2 gradient stated. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: Abstract is an evolutionary review of invasive placentation; it discusses spiral-artery trophoblast invasion qualitatively but gives no spiral-artery PO2 value. Rationale states the value is estimated indirectly and is model-dependent.
checks.note: No PO2 figure in abstract; indirect model estimate. Fulltext unavailable.

checks.source_reports: The abstract discusses GLUT1 expression and activity qualitatively but reports no maximum transport rate (Vmax) and no per-area flux figure.
checks.note: Abstract-only; no Vmax value present. Stored value is an acknowledged Tier-C extrapolation not quantified in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: Brown 2011 abstract reports relative GLUT3 protein expression across gestation (2nd trimester 48 +/- 7% of 1st; 3rd trimester 34 +/- 10% of 1st) but gives no absolute Vmax or per-area flux value.
checks.supporting_quote: GLUT3 expression in microvillous membranes detected by Western blot decreased through the trimesters such that expression in the second trimester (wks 14-26) was 48 ± 7% of that in the first trimester and by the third trimester (wks 31-40) only 34 ± 10% of first trimester expression.
checks.note: Only relative expression percentages reported; no absolute mmol/min/m^2 Vmax. Stored value is an acknowledged Tier-C inference from relative expression.

checks.source_reports: Hutcheon abstract reports placental-weight z-score associations with perinatal outcomes (e.g., stillbirth OR 2.0); contains no allometric PW=a*FW^b regression coefficient.
checks.note: Allometric prefactor is derived; the abstract reports outcome odds ratios, not a regression prefactor.

checks.source_reports: Hutcheon abstract; reports no allometric exponent for the placental-fetal weight relation.
checks.note: Exponent is a derived fit; abstract contains only z-score outcome statistics.

checks.source_reports: Review abstract of evolutionary factors in placental oxygen transfer; reports no logistic growth-rate coefficient for villous surface-area expansion.
checks.note: Logistic fit parameter; the cited abstract contains no numeric growth-rate value (notes themselves state 'not reported directly in source').

checks.source_reports: Morphomics review abstract; reports no intervillous-space volume figure.
checks.note: No numeric intervillous volume in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: Carter evolutionary review abstract; reports no gestational week for a villous-area logistic midpoint.
checks.note: Logistic-curve inflection week; notes state 'not reported directly in source.'

checks.source_reports: Pijnenborg 'facts and controversies' abstract discusses spiral-artery remodelling qualitatively; reports no count of spiral arteries.
checks.note: No numeric spiral-artery count in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: Pijnenborg abstract describes spiral arteries being remodelled into highly dilated vessels (qualitative); reports no diameter in mm.
checks.note: Abstract qualitatively notes dilation but gives no numeric diameter.

checks.source_reports: Morphomics review abstract; reports no syncytiotrophoblast thickness figure.
checks.note: No numeric syncytial-layer thickness in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: Morphomics review abstract; reports no total villous-capillary length figure.
checks.note: No numeric capillary-length figure in the abstract.

checks.source_reports: Bianchi 1996 abstract is qualitative ('rare nucleated fetal cells circulate within maternal blood') and reports detection rates by PCR; it gives no cells/mL concentration.
checks.note: No cells/mL concentration in the abstract; Tier D hypothesis-only value cannot be verified against the source text.
